paragraphs.body: A Home Not Seen Before Growing up my coach was as imposing as a giant. Not because he was tall or anything, rather he is a mere 5 foot 2 inches. But most people forget that the most imposing part of a person is their personality. Through his thick Bronx accent mixed into his made-up slang of word’s that I am sure don’t come up in the dictionary often. Slang is the use of word’s that are informal and more common in writing than speech (slang). And my coach used loads of it. He was just so loud and intense as if his personality was borderline insane. He would not stop talking, even worse my young self was scared I was being taught by a man with ill intent. Merely because of his diction, which consisted of all the things I would hear in a Kay Flock or Asap Ferg song. Otherwise known as affiliated gang rappers who sing lyrics such as “he get shot if he totin’ on Kelly (grrah grrah)” (Kay). I mean would you even understand what he’s getting at? Me neither. But this tough character and I communicate as efficiently as two objects. I couldn’t understand why until I was let go of my parent’s leash to explore the city myself. I was able to wander the street’s freely, and trust me they isn’t pretty. New York is indescribable to anyone who never lived there. Because when I tell you about the crackhead who uses my sidewalk as a toilet and recites his best version of Mariah Carey at 3 am you would be astonished. Or when I ride my bike down first avenue and there are two individuals fighting each other over a sandwich. Even when I am at school I leave and I see a man in an electric wheelchair who thinks he is a car, a car that doesn’t abide by the law because I saw him ride in the parallel to an avenue and someone got Joshua 2 across unharmed. In contrast I can run through Central park and enjoy nature. Or join the millions of tourists downtown and shop in one of New Yorks most wealthy neighborhoods called Soho. Furthermore, I could go to Time Square where you will be in awe of the number of lights around you, and how you can dream you’ll be there too. To me New York is the most beautiful place on earth, not because it’s perfect, no far from it. But because within the 200 different languages spoken and nearly 25% of non-speaking English local’s according to (NYC Department of City Planning/ Language) it is the most unthinkable mixture of culture that makes a whole new identity that belongs to the city that never stops.

paragraphs.body: For me I remember New York is special. Because beyond the locations and bizarre occurrences they are matched by the most intriguing and special events. Whether it is a Macy’s parade, thrift pop up shop, Broadway theater, flash mobs, ferry rides, yellow taxi adventures, subway joyrides and my personal favorite the Winter Village. You will never stop moving because there’s always something to catch in New York. Not to mention the countless restaurant and food spots. I could go to queens and get some of Latin America’s best tasting food for the cheapest prices. Take a trip to Dumbo in Brooklyn and find some of the best new mixture’s of culture’s, or even the best bagels. I could take the ferry to Manhattan and get some of the world’s best pizza. All while hearing some of New Yorks finest and long history of rap and jazz classics. New York is an endlessly realm of possibilities, there is just countless thing’s to do. But the thing I loved the most is the diversity. I speak Spanish at home, but in New York you can speak it anywhere. Even my grandmother who only speaks Spanish manages to go about her day just fine because Spanish is everywhere. You can find it in the forms of restaurants or events but mainly just by walking down the street, Spanish is all over. By sharing this language with millions of others it makes me feel as if I belong to a community. Not many people see this side Joshua 3 of New York from a far. In reality, its one of the most important attributes of New York, the Hispanic community and anybody that speaks Spanish gives me a taste of my childhood, my home country and identity. The experience is unique for everyone, but through the differences I call can confidently call it home. It’s the uniqueness that makes you a new Yorker.

paragraphs.body: That mixture of culture and tradition imbed in my identity through language and foreigners can tell. Every time I told someone I live in New York I get the most astonished and question filled replies to a person can have. They go in awe about how that’s their dream and how that is where everybody wishes to live there. For example, my college roommate’s parents took me out for a lunch one afternoon and they could not believe where I came from. They spoke all about Manhattan and I replied in a upbeat tone and modest responses to make a lasting impression. Once my roommate began to speak to me directly my slang began to come out. I think everyone has seen a parent’s face when they are disappointed. You could tell they thought I sounded like someone of poor education and social standing. They began to question me about where that language originated from and they were in shock about how I learned it through other kids my age and how prominent it was in New York. In fact I decided to educate them about the city and how there is more than Wall street and Times Square. I told them you can search up Subway creatures and find banned animals traveling to their 9-5 with their owner or large amounts of gang violence dispersed in even the richest parts of the city covered or how I have an average of 1 interaction with a crackhead a day and no, not by choice. They had no words, “how could this be?” the father asked. I explained to him the mass immigration and diversity of the city creates new traditions and communication. Hence the slang I use often originates where most of these very overpopulated and diverse areas such as low-income housing which spreads throughout the city. In addition to the very random things you see within the city is just people who come from Joshua 4 different backgrounds, norms and cultures expressing themselves. It is now imbedded into my identity to be more open minded and accepting of cultures because I lived around so many cultures. In fact, I also learned word’s in Arabic, French and Persian. The slang mixes from all over the place including my native language Spanish. This blend tends to be new to everyone, but we embrace it and create a new culture. No matter the social standing you can learn New York slang or even another language. By speaking spanish I contribute to the diversity which resides within my identity. But the parent’s could barely believe, and most people wouldn’t either. It is a blatant reminder of how the city is marketed and shown to others. Only the bright lights and beautiful parts but most will never know about the true beauty which comes from the parts that not many want to show.

paragraphs.body: “A New Home” Hello Grandmother, I just came from Brooklyn there was a shooting. No son, Don’t go too far, you are not allowed out anymore Hello Grandmother, A snake was on my train car, he almost bit someone son, stay alert this city is a very crazy place full of danger Hello grandmother, me and my friends saw a man get mugged Son, you look okay, It happens just stay alert of your surroundings. Hello grandmother, I saw a monkey riding a bike on the bus Son, that quite special, only here can we find something like that Hello grandmother, a group are hosting a massive Halloween flashmob want to come? Joshua 5 It’s our city now, I wouldn’t miss it “How it feel’s to be home” All in all, even though the perspective on New York may be true its not the whole truth. The value of living in New York isn’t in its architecture but about the way it shapes your identity to jump through hoops and accept change of identity. My identity has developed so much in New York. Remember that crazy coach I had, I can even speak to my former coach is the slang and tone he uses to have a complete conversation. A man who seemed so intimidating now wasn’t, but in fact I turned intimidating to foreigners myself. That comes to show how much identity can change. I manage to learn from other culture’s including the slang from New York native’s and in a sense, I have become one too. Likewise, by me sharing my Spanish I help someone become something new. My experience in New York shows the power of language and the role it plays in identity. Through language I can become someone new, add to our identity and grow with it. I personally overcame this through living in New York overtime, and having an open attitude toward learning and changing my identity I believe in any community someone can influence it by contributing their identity. Connecting back to how New Yorkers have over 200 languages spoken between us new Yorkers (language), every place in the world has some sort of diversity. In a sense this makes identity a never-ending development cycle. I love New York not because it showed me how to be a part of a community that everyone can be a part of through the power of language and their identity. Now how will you change your world to develop a new identity within yourself?
